did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a pre-trained AlexNet deep convolutional neural network for feature extraction and an ECOC SVM classifier to classify four types of skin cancer (Actinic Keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, and Melanoma) from RGB images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the collection of 3753 RGB skin cancer images, preprocessing, feature extraction using a pre-trained AlexNet deep convolutional neural network, and classification using an ECOC SVM classifier. It reports specific implementation results, including accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics for four types of skin cancer, and compares these results with previous studies, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: The skin cancer classification using deep convolutional neural network
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of four types of skin cancer—Actinic Keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, and Melanoma—using a deep learning approach. RGB images were collected from the internet and pre-processed by cropping to reduce noise before being fed into a pre-trained AlexNet deep convolutional neural network for feature extraction. An Error-Correcting Output Codes (ECOC) Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier was then utilized to categorize the extracted features. The model was evaluated on a dataset of 3,753 images, with performance measured using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics. The results demonstrated high performance, with average accuracy values ranging from 91.8% to 95.1% across the different cancer types. Comparative analysis indicated that the proposed method achieved higher accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity than several previous studies, particularly for melanoma classification.

all_task_types.target: Four discrete skin cancer diagnostic categories: Actinic Keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, and Melanoma.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on classifying skin cancer into four distinct classes using an ECOC SVM classifier with features extracted from a pre-trained AlexNet deep convolutional neural network. The task is multiclass classification of RGB images. Performance metrics reported include accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for each class.

datasets_used.name: Internet-collected Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of RGB skin cancer images collected from related Internet sites. The images were pre-processed by cropping to reduce noise (e.g., other organs or tools). The dataset contains four types of skin cancers.

use.training: Used to train the ECOC SVM classifier using features extracted by the pre-trained AlexNet deep convolutional neural network.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the classification performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) of the proposed model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, and compared qualitatively with previous studies.

Internet-collected Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: The study compared the proposed method against previous works for Melanoma classification. The proposed study achieved higher accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity than Esteva et al. [4], Blum et al. [5], Almansour et al. [10], Isasi et al. [14], Anas et al. [17], and Kiran et al. [20]. Specificity was lower than Ruiz et al. [8], Hitoshi et al. [15], and Abbas et al. [19], but their sensitivity was noted as very low.

architecture_used.main_model: AlexNet (pre-trained deep convolutional neural network) combined with an Error-Correcting Output Codes Support Vector Machine (ECOC SVM) classifier

conclusions: The proposed method using a pre-trained AlexNet deep convolutional neural network for feature extraction and an ECOC SVM classifier effectively classifies four types of skin cancer (Actinic Keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, and Melanoma) from RGB images.
The model achieved high performance with average accuracy values ranging from 91.8% to 95.1%, sensitivity from 96.9% to 98.9%, and specificity from 90.74% to 94.17% across the different cancer types.

The proposed study demonstrated higher accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for melanoma classification compared to previous works by Esteva et al., Blum et al., Almansour et al., Isasi et al., Anas et al., and Kiran et al.
While some previous studies (Ruiz et al., Hitoshi et al., Abbas et al.) reported higher specificity, their sensitivity values were noted to be very low.
The pre-trained AlexNet model is suitable for classifying small and medium-sized datasets of skin cancer images.
The high performance for Squamous Cell Carcinoma is attributed to the dataset collection method, where these images were clearer and more similar to each other compared to other cancer types.
Future work will focus on extending the classification based on the ABCD rule and developing a smartphone application for skin cancer classification.
important_remarks: Dataset bias: Images were collected from the Internet, which may introduce selection bias and lack of clinical diversity compared to standardized dermoscopic datasets.
Limited external validation: The study relies on a single, self-collected dataset of 3,753 images without independent external validation or cross-validation across diverse clinical sites.

Comparison limitations: Comparative results with previous works (e.g., Esteva et al.) are potentially unfair due to differences in dataset composition, image quality (RGB vs. dermoscopic), and classification tasks (multiclass vs. binary melanoma).
Performance variability: Accuracy ranges widely from 82.17% to 98.1% across classes, indicating inconsistent performance, particularly for Actinic Keratoses.

Sensitivity-Specificity trade-off: While the proposed method shows high sensitivity, some previous studies (Ruiz et al., Hitoshi et al., Abbas et al.) reported higher specificity, though with very low sensitivity.

No statistical significance testing: The paper reports point estimates for accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity but does not provide confidence intervals or p-values to assess statistical significance.
Hardware constraints: Experiments were conducted on modest hardware (Intel Core i7, 8GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce GT 540 M), which may limit reproducibility or scalability for larger datasets.
Preprocessing simplicity: Only cropping was used to reduce noise; no advanced augmentation or normalization techniques were employed, which may limit robustness.
